Template code for logistic regression and ggplot (for bee data analysis)

Leslie Ng

2025-05-07

Install required packages

install.packages("lme4") # linear mixed effect models
install.packages("ggplot2") # plot graphs
install.packages("tidyverse") # QOL features
install.packages("wesanderson") # color palette
install.packages("dplyr")  # data manipulation
install.packages("BFpack") # Bayes Factor for logistic regression
install.packages("ggThemeAssist") # Add-in to customize ggplot2 themes
install.packages("performance") # For assumption checking
install.packages("DHARMa") # For assumption checking
install.packages("MuMIn") # For dredge function
install.packages("emmeans") # For Posthoc analysis

Load packages

library(lme4) # linear mixed effect models
library(ggplot2) # plot graphs
library(tidyverse) # QOL features
library(wesanderson) # color palette
library(dplyr)  # data manipulation
library(BFpack) # Bayes Factor for logistic regression
library(ggThemeAssist) # you may need to restart you session for this add-in to appear
library(performance) # For assumption checking
library(DHARMa) # For assumption checking
library(MuMIn) # For dredge function
library(emmeans) # For Posthoc analysis

Data importing

Import your data, then treat columns as factor (Bee ID, condition, Block) as they are categorical. ‘Block’ is only used for generating line graphs, if you don’t intend to do this you may ignore this. When running ‘View’, notice how the data is in long format. This means that there is a row for each choice in your data, and this is the format that is required to run an R analysis (as opposed to a wide format where there is a column for each choice). Note in the importing code “.” means located in the folder where this script is located, whereas “..” means located one file structure above where this script is located.

# Data importing -----------------------------------------------------------------------

test <- read.csv("../Data/test.csv", header=TRUE)
training <- read.csv("../Data/training.csv", header=TRUE)
summary(test) #view summary
str(test) #display structure
summary(training)
str(test)
View(test) # see above text

# Data wrangling ------------------------------------------------------------------------

training$BEEID <- as.factor(training$BEEID) # treat as a categorical factor
training$CONDITION <- as.factor(training$CONDITION)
training$BLOCK <- as.factor(training$BLOCK)
summary(training)
test$BEEID <- as.factor(test$BEEID) # treat as a categorical factor
test$CONDITION <- as.factor(test$CONDITION)
summary(test)

ytraining <-subset(training, CONDITION == "YELLOW") #subset original data sets into data including only the yellow or blue condition
mean(ytraining$CHOICE)
btraining <-subset(training, CONDITION == "BLUE")
mean(btraining$CHOICE)
ytest <- subset(test, CONDITION == "YELLOW")
mean(ytest$CHOICE)
btest <- subset(test, CONDITION == "BLUE")
mean(btest$CHOICE)

Logistic regressions

The data set includes data collected from an experiment where bees were individually trained to discriminate blue vs yellow stimuli. In the training phase, bees experienced 20 choices towards the stimuli and were rewarded or punished depending on whether they made a correct or incorrect choice. Afterwards, bees experienced a test phase where they made 10 unrewarded choices towards yellow or blue stimuli to test if they had learnt the task without the presence of the reward or punishment.

Training data

Let’s first analyse the training data. Let’s first look at a simple model only looking at the blue condition subset data to keep things simple. As our data is binary, we have to use a generalized linear model with a logit link function. This link function transforms our binary choices (1 and 0) to real numbers from negative infinity to infinity. As we want to include Bee ID as a random factor to control for variation within subjects. The model structure is quite simple, we want to know the effect of trial number on choice (i.e. does increasing trial number affect the choices made by bees):

btraininglm <- glmer(CHOICE~ TRIAL + (1|BEEID), family = binomial, data = btraining) 
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
summary(btraininglm)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: CHOICE ~ TRIAL + (1 | BEEID)
##    Data: btraining
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    203.8    213.7    -98.9    197.8      197 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.5477  0.2819  0.3949  0.5614  0.9018 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  BEEID  (Intercept) 0        0       
## Number of obs: 200, groups:  BEEID, 10
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)  0.08420    0.32797   0.257 0.797387    
## TRIAL        0.12242    0.03295   3.715 0.000203 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##       (Intr)
## TRIAL -0.843
## optimizer (Nelder_Mead) convergence code: 0 (OK)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')

Also note the structure of the random effect: (1|BEEID). The ‘1’ here indicates that this is a random intercept model. In other words, our model assumes the slope or effect of the predictor to be the same across all bees, but their intercepts will vary depending on the individual. For example, we assume that each bee has a different baseline probability of choosing the correct choice, some bees are smarter than the others. The alternative is a random slope term (TRIAL|BEEID) which additionally assumes that the effect of trial AND the intercept both different between individuals. Whilst this is usually true in reality, many of our models only use a random intercept due to the complexity of random intercept models and lead to problems in convergence or overfitting.

summary(btraininglm) # view models

Notice that you will get an error message “boundary (singular) fit: see help(‘isSingular’)”. This happens when one of the variance components is estimated as zero. This typically has one of two explanations:

  1. The random effects structure is over-fitted - usually because of too many random slopes
  2. One of more variance components is actually very close to zero and there is insufficient data to estimate it above zero.

In many cases it is likely that the second explanation is true and you must decide whether to keep or remove the random effect. In my opinion it is better to present a simpler model if there is no change to the result, but if you do this make sure to explain this decision. In this case, a simplified model would look like this:

btraininglm2 <- glm(CHOICE~ TRIAL, family = binomial, data = btraining) 
summary(btraininglm)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: CHOICE ~ TRIAL + (1 | BEEID)
##    Data: btraining
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    203.8    213.7    -98.9    197.8      197 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.5477  0.2819  0.3949  0.5614  0.9018 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  BEEID  (Intercept) 0        0       
## Number of obs: 200, groups:  BEEID, 10
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)  0.08420    0.32797   0.257 0.797387    
## TRIAL        0.12242    0.03295   3.715 0.000203 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##       (Intr)
## TRIAL -0.843
## optimizer (Nelder_Mead) convergence code: 0 (OK)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
ytraininglm2 <- glm(CHOICE~ TRIAL, family = binomial, data = ytraining)

For illustrative purposes, this is what the raw data looks alongside your regression line:

ggplot(btraining, aes(x = TRIAL, y = CHOICE)) +

  # raw binary data (0s and 1s)
  geom_jitter(height = 0.05, width = 0.2, alpha = 0.4) +

  # logistic S-curve from model
  stat_smooth(method = "glm",
              method.args = list(family = "binomial"),
              se = TRUE) +

  scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1)) +

  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0,1)) +

  labs(x = "Trial",
       y = "Choice (0 = incorrect, 1 = correct)") +

  theme_classic()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

In this example I have decided to separately analyse bees trained to blue. However, most of the time you want to analyse the full model to get the ‘full picture’ of how all your variables affect the response altogether. This is generally recommended if your data allows for it as it allows for interaction effects to be examined:

traininglm <- glm(CHOICE~ TRIAL+CONDITION+TRIAL*CONDITION, family = binomial, data = training) 
summary(traininglm) # Note that the interaction term is not significant, therefore we will drop it
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = CHOICE ~ TRIAL + CONDITION + TRIAL * CONDITION, 
##     family = binomial, data = training)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                       Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)            0.08420    0.32796   0.257 0.797387    
## TRIAL                  0.12242    0.03295   3.715 0.000203 ***
## CONDITIONYELLOW        0.22762    0.44318   0.514 0.607533    
## TRIAL:CONDITIONYELLOW -0.11745    0.04132  -2.842 0.004481 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 499.98  on 399  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 468.49  on 396  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 476.49
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

Dredge

What should you do when your model is quite complex and you’re not sure which variables or interactions to include? The dredge function helps evaluate all possible combinations of predictor variables and interactions to help determine which model is the best fit for your data. Dredge then ranks your models based on AICc.For example, here I included a few other variables in my full model, then the dredge function should tell you that the intercept only model is the best fit. The ‘+’ symbol indicates what you should keep in the model and a blank space means that the model ommited the variable.

Note, when selecting which predictors to keep, you should largely make choices based on your existing hypothesis e.g. theres no point in cutting temperature if your study is looking at temperature effects specifically.

  

Full_model <- glmer(CHOICE~  + TRIAL + TEMPERATURE + URBAN_GRAD + TEMPERATURE*URBAN_GRAD*TRIAL + (1|BEEID), family = binomial, data = btest) 
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
summary(Full_model)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: 
## CHOICE ~ +TRIAL + TEMPERATURE + URBAN_GRAD + TEMPERATURE * URBAN_GRAD *  
##     TRIAL + (1 | BEEID)
##    Data: btest
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    154.3    205.6    -58.2    116.3       91 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.0220 -1.0076  0.4960  0.6755  1.0622 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  BEEID  (Intercept) 0        0       
## Number of obs: 110, groups:  BEEID, 11
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                                               Estimate Std. Error z value
## (Intercept)                                     0.1799     1.5781   0.114
## TRIAL                                           0.2457     0.3094   0.794
## TEMPERATURELOW                                 -0.1126     2.1367  -0.053
## TEMPERATUREMODERATE                             0.4840     1.9093   0.254
## URBAN_GRADMODERATE                             -0.3285     1.8572  -0.177
## URBAN_GRADURBAN                               255.5099   218.4359   1.170
## TEMPERATURELOW:URBAN_GRADMODERATE               0.4411     2.8311   0.156
## TEMPERATUREMODERATE:URBAN_GRADMODERATE         -0.7682     2.5612  -0.300
## TEMPERATURELOW:URBAN_GRADURBAN               -254.5696   218.4483  -1.165
## TEMPERATUREMODERATE:URBAN_GRADURBAN          -256.1066   218.4426  -1.172
## TRIAL:TEMPERATURELOW                           -0.0973     0.3981  -0.244
## TRIAL:TEMPERATUREMODERATE                      -0.1641     0.3591  -0.457
## TRIAL:URBAN_GRADMODERATE                       -0.1434     0.3493  -0.410
## TRIAL:URBAN_GRADURBAN                         -34.3445   316.1845  -0.109
## TRIAL:TEMPERATURELOW:URBAN_GRADMODERATE         0.2407     0.5296   0.454
## TRIAL:TEMPERATUREMODERATE:URBAN_GRADMODERATE    0.2179     0.4586   0.475
## TRIAL:TEMPERATURELOW:URBAN_GRADURBAN           34.1672   316.1846   0.108
## TRIAL:TEMPERATUREMODERATE:URBAN_GRADURBAN      34.4113   316.1845   0.109
##                                              Pr(>|z|)
## (Intercept)                                     0.909
## TRIAL                                           0.427
## TEMPERATURELOW                                  0.958
## TEMPERATUREMODERATE                             0.800
## URBAN_GRADMODERATE                              0.860
## URBAN_GRADURBAN                                 0.242
## TEMPERATURELOW:URBAN_GRADMODERATE               0.876
## TEMPERATUREMODERATE:URBAN_GRADMODERATE          0.764
## TEMPERATURELOW:URBAN_GRADURBAN                  0.244
## TEMPERATUREMODERATE:URBAN_GRADURBAN             0.241
## TRIAL:TEMPERATURELOW                            0.807
## TRIAL:TEMPERATUREMODERATE                       0.648
## TRIAL:URBAN_GRADMODERATE                        0.681
## TRIAL:URBAN_GRADURBAN                           0.914
## TRIAL:TEMPERATURELOW:URBAN_GRADMODERATE         0.650
## TRIAL:TEMPERATUREMODERATE:URBAN_GRADMODERATE    0.635
## TRIAL:TEMPERATURELOW:URBAN_GRADURBAN            0.914
## TRIAL:TEMPERATUREMODERATE:URBAN_GRADURBAN       0.913
## 
## Correlation matrix not shown by default, as p = 18 > 12.
## Use print(x, correlation=TRUE)  or
##     vcov(x)        if you need it
## optimizer (Nelder_Mead) convergence code: 0 (OK)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
options(na.action = na.fail)
glmdredge <- dredge(Full_model)
## Fixed term is "(Intercept)"
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
glmdredge
## Global model call: glmer(formula = CHOICE ~ +TRIAL + TEMPERATURE + URBAN_GRAD + 
##     TEMPERATURE * URBAN_GRAD * TRIAL + (1 | BEEID), data = btest, 
##     family = binomial)
## ---
## Model selection table 
##      (Int) TEM      TRI URB_GRA TEM:TRI TEM:URB_GRA TRI:URB_GRA TEM:TRI:URB_GRA
## 1   0.8473                                                                     
## 3   0.5764      0.05001                                                        
## 5   1.0990                    +                                                
## 2   0.7309   +                                                                 
## 7   0.8264      0.05045       +                                                
## 4   0.4588   +  0.05014                                                        
## 39  0.4068      0.13230       +                               +                
## 6   1.0460   +                +                                                
## 12  1.0010   + -0.04849               +                                        
## 8   0.7732   +  0.05055       +                                                
## 40  0.3513   +  0.13250       +                               +                
## 16  1.3240   + -0.04890       +       +                                        
## 48  1.1430   + -0.01092       +       +                       +                
## 22  1.3860   +                +                   +                            
## 24  1.1110   +  0.05124       +                   +                            
## 56  0.6971   +  0.13320       +                   +           +                
## 32  1.6680   + -0.05007       +       +           +                            
## 64  1.5790   + -0.03444       +       +           +           +                
## 128 0.1799   +  0.24570       +       +           +           +               +
##     df  logLik  AICc delta weight
## 1    2 -67.195 138.5  0.00  0.500
## 3    3 -66.958 140.1  1.64  0.220
## 5    4 -66.717 141.8  3.31  0.095
## 2    4 -67.055 142.5  3.99  0.068
## 7    5 -66.478 143.5  5.03  0.040
## 4    5 -66.818 144.2  5.71  0.029
## 39   7 -64.813 144.7  6.22  0.022
## 6    6 -66.605 146.0  7.52  0.012
## 12   7 -66.245 147.6  9.08  0.005
## 8    7 -66.365 147.8  9.33  0.005
## 40   9 -64.696 149.2 10.69  0.002
## 16   9 -65.787 151.4 12.87  0.001
## 48  11 -63.942 152.6 14.08  0.000
## 22  10 -65.880 154.0 15.48  0.000
## 24  11 -65.637 156.0 17.46  0.000
## 56  13 -63.948 157.7 19.19  0.000
## 32  13 -65.055 159.9 21.40  0.000
## 64  15 -63.096 161.3 22.80  0.000
## 128 19 -58.154 162.8 24.25  0.000
## Models ranked by AICc(x) 
## Random terms (all models): 
##   1 | BEEID
options(na.action = na.omit)

 

Assumption checking

Remember to check model assumptions for each model. This can be done use the ‘performance’ package with the following code:

performance::check_model(traininglm, check = c("qq", "outliers","reqq","binned_residuals")) 

binned_residuals(traininglm)
## Ok: About 95% of the residuals are inside the error bounds.
check_convergence(traininglm)
## `check_convergence()` does not work for models of class 'glm'.
check_overdispersion(traininglm)
## # Overdispersion test
## 
##  dispersion ratio = 1.006
##           p-value = 0.944
## No overdispersion detected.
modperm<- model_performance(traininglm)
display(modperm, format = "markdown", digits = 2, caption = NULL)
AIC AICc BIC Tjur’s R2 RMSE Sigma Log_loss Score_log Score_spherical PCP
476.49 476.59 492.45 0.07 0.45 1.00 0.59 -340.59 7.08e-03 0.60
Note that for binomial data, the residuals can’t follow a normal distribution as they are restricted to the interval [0,1]. If comparing between models, use the AIC to select the best model (models with the lowest scores are better). See https://easystats.github.io/performance/#comprehensive-visualization-of-model-checks for further details. Also note that if you are using an intercept only model then you don’t really need to check for assumptions as you are mainly focussing on the intercept term and it’s confidence interval.

 

We also should check the linearity assumption for logistic regression: there should be a roughly linear relationship between the predictor and the log odds of an outcome. We can simply graph this and see if there is a linear relationship.

probabilities <- predict(btraininglm, type = "response")
logit = log(probabilities/(1-probabilities))

ggplot(btraining, aes(TRIAL, logit)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.5, colour = "black") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess") +
  theme_bw()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

 

Finally, We can use the DHARMa package to assess the goodness of fit of the model. DHARMa does this by simulating residuals from your model and comparing these residuals with your actual observed residuals. This will help identify if your model has a good fit with the observed data.

simulated_res <- simulateResiduals(fittedModel = traininglm)
plot(simulated_res)

These graphs will also help you identify deviations from normality assumptions. If there is nothing wrong with the simulated residuals, it means that the observed data likely originated from the fitted model. Specifically, a flat residual distribution shows that your data fits the model. See: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/DHARMa/vignettes/DHARMa.html for further details.

 

Transformations

Sometimes you may have to transform your data if your model does not meet its assumptions and your predictor variable is continuous e.g. duration. The following are some simple transformations you can try. After you try each one, run the assumption tests again to see if this has fixed the problem.

btest$log_RESPONSE_TIME <- log(btest$RESPONSE_TIME) # log transform
btest$sqrt_RESPONSE_TIME <- sqrt(btest$RESPONSE_TIME) # square root
btest$inv_RESPONSE_TIME <- 1/btest$RESPONSE_TIME # inverse transformation

 

Posthoc investigation of interactions

If you find a significant interaction effect between two variables which are both factors with multiple levels, it is important to conduct posthoc analyses to help interpret this interaction. For example, the emmeans R package allows you to calculate estimated marginal means (EMMs), which are model-based predicted values of the response for each level or combination of predictors, while accounting for the other variables in the model.

To illustrate this let’s generate a hypothetical model

Hypo_model <- glmer(CHOICE~  + TEMPERATURE*URBAN_GRAD + (1|BEEID), family = binomial, data = btest) 
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')

 

Now in this example, TEMPERATURE is either HIGH, MODERATE or LOW, and URBAN_GRAD is either LOW, MODERATE, or URBAN. If there was an interaction between TEMPERATURE and URBAN_GRAD, then emmeans helps you break this down by asking questions such as “What is the effect of TEMPERATURE within each URBAN_GRAD level?” or conversely “What is the effect of URBAN_GRAD at each level of TEMPERATURE?”. Rather than calculating raw means from the data, emmeans uses the fitted model to generate predicted values, which represent the expected response (e.g., probability in a logistic regression) for each combination of conditions. This makes it easier to interpret the interaction in terms of differences in predicted outcomes between groups.

emmeans(Hypo_model, pairwise ~ TEMPERATURE | URBAN_GRAD) # What is the effect if temperature within each urban gradient e.g. if urban gradient is low, what is the difference between high and low temperature bees
## $emmeans
## URBAN_GRAD = LOW:
##  TEMPERATURE emmean    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  HIGH         1.386 0.791 Inf   -0.1632      2.94
##  LOW          0.847 0.690 Inf   -0.5052      2.20
##  MODERATE     1.099 0.516 Inf    0.0865      2.11
## 
## URBAN_GRAD = MODERATE:
##  TEMPERATURE emmean    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  HIGH         0.405 0.456 Inf   -0.4891      1.30
##  LOW          1.386 0.791 Inf   -0.1632      2.94
##  MODERATE     0.405 0.645 Inf   -0.8597      1.67
## 
## URBAN_GRAD = URBAN:
##  TEMPERATURE emmean    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  HIGH         0.847 0.690 Inf   -0.5052      2.20
##  LOW          0.847 0.690 Inf   -0.5052      2.20
##  MODERATE     0.847 0.690 Inf   -0.5052      2.20
## 
## Results are given on the logit (not the response) scale. 
## Confidence level used: 0.95 
## 
## $contrasts
## URBAN_GRAD = LOW:
##  contrast        estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  HIGH - LOW         0.539 1.050 Inf   0.514  0.8647
##  HIGH - MODERATE    0.288 0.944 Inf   0.305  0.9501
##  LOW - MODERATE    -0.251 0.862 Inf  -0.292  0.9542
## 
## URBAN_GRAD = MODERATE:
##  contrast        estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  HIGH - LOW        -0.981 0.913 Inf  -1.074  0.5300
##  HIGH - MODERATE    0.000 0.791 Inf   0.000  1.0000
##  LOW - MODERATE     0.981 1.020 Inf   0.961  0.6016
## 
## URBAN_GRAD = URBAN:
##  contrast        estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  HIGH - LOW         0.000 0.976 Inf   0.000  1.0000
##  HIGH - MODERATE    0.000 0.976 Inf   0.000  1.0000
##  LOW - MODERATE     0.000 0.976 Inf   0.000  1.0000
## 
## Results are given on the log odds ratio (not the response) scale. 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 3 estimates
emmeans(Hypo_model, pairwise ~ URBAN_GRAD | TEMPERATURE) # What is the effect of urban gradient within each temperature level
## $emmeans
## TEMPERATURE = HIGH:
##  URBAN_GRAD emmean    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  LOW         1.386 0.791 Inf   -0.1632      2.94
##  MODERATE    0.405 0.456 Inf   -0.4891      1.30
##  URBAN       0.847 0.690 Inf   -0.5052      2.20
## 
## TEMPERATURE = LOW:
##  URBAN_GRAD emmean    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  LOW         0.847 0.690 Inf   -0.5052      2.20
##  MODERATE    1.386 0.791 Inf   -0.1632      2.94
##  URBAN       0.847 0.690 Inf   -0.5052      2.20
## 
## TEMPERATURE = MODERATE:
##  URBAN_GRAD emmean    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  LOW         1.099 0.516 Inf    0.0865      2.11
##  MODERATE    0.405 0.645 Inf   -0.8597      1.67
##  URBAN       0.847 0.690 Inf   -0.5052      2.20
## 
## Results are given on the logit (not the response) scale. 
## Confidence level used: 0.95 
## 
## $contrasts
## TEMPERATURE = HIGH:
##  contrast         estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  LOW - MODERATE      0.981 0.913 Inf   1.074  0.5300
##  LOW - URBAN         0.539 1.050 Inf   0.514  0.8647
##  MODERATE - URBAN   -0.442 0.827 Inf  -0.534  0.8546
## 
## TEMPERATURE = LOW:
##  contrast         estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  LOW - MODERATE     -0.539 1.050 Inf  -0.514  0.8647
##  LOW - URBAN         0.000 0.976 Inf   0.000  1.0000
##  MODERATE - URBAN    0.539 1.050 Inf   0.514  0.8647
## 
## TEMPERATURE = MODERATE:
##  contrast         estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  LOW - MODERATE      0.693 0.827 Inf   0.839  0.6790
##  LOW - URBAN         0.251 0.862 Inf   0.292  0.9542
##  MODERATE - URBAN   -0.442 0.945 Inf  -0.468  0.8865
## 
## Results are given on the log odds ratio (not the response) scale. 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 3 estimates
plot(emmeans(Hypo_model, ~ TEMPERATURE * URBAN_GRAD, type = "response")) # Visualize, the model-estimated probability of a correct choice for each combination of temperature and urban gradient level.

If you have one categorical and one continuous variable as an interaction, you could also use emmeans by converting your continuous variable into categories. Alternatively use the emtrends function to compare if slopes differ significantly from each other?

Hypo_model2 <- glmer(CHOICE~  + TRIAL*URBAN_GRAD + (1|BEEID), family = binomial, data = btest) 
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
emmeans(Hypo_model2, 
        pairwise ~ URBAN_GRAD | TRIAL,
        at = list(TRIAL = c(1, 10, 20))) # What is the effect of urban gradient at Trial 1, 10 or 20
## $emmeans
## TRIAL =  1:
##  URBAN_GRAD  emmean    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  LOW         0.5391 0.639 Inf  -0.71416      1.79
##  MODERATE    0.0285 0.601 Inf  -1.15005      1.21
##  URBAN       1.6570 0.844 Inf   0.00294      3.31
## 
## TRIAL = 10:
##  URBAN_GRAD  emmean    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  LOW         1.7297 0.759 Inf   0.24214      3.22
##  MODERATE    1.2552 0.669 Inf  -0.05509      2.57
##  URBAN       0.1312 0.708 Inf  -1.25718      1.52
## 
## TRIAL = 20:
##  URBAN_GRAD  emmean    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  LOW         3.0526 2.010 Inf  -0.89231      7.00
##  MODERATE    2.6181 1.800 Inf  -0.91702      6.15
##  URBAN      -1.5641 2.080 Inf  -5.64788      2.52
## 
## Results are given on the logit (not the response) scale. 
## Confidence level used: 0.95 
## 
## $contrasts
## TRIAL =  1:
##  contrast         estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  LOW - MODERATE      0.511 0.878 Inf   0.582  0.8299
##  LOW - URBAN        -1.118 1.060 Inf  -1.056  0.5416
##  MODERATE - URBAN   -1.628 1.040 Inf  -1.572  0.2580
## 
## TRIAL = 10:
##  contrast         estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  LOW - MODERATE      0.475 1.010 Inf   0.469  0.8858
##  LOW - URBAN         1.598 1.040 Inf   1.540  0.2724
##  MODERATE - URBAN    1.124 0.974 Inf   1.154  0.4809
## 
## TRIAL = 20:
##  contrast         estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  LOW - MODERATE      0.434 2.700 Inf   0.161  0.9859
##  LOW - URBAN         4.617 2.900 Inf   1.594  0.2483
##  MODERATE - URBAN    4.182 2.760 Inf   1.518  0.2826
## 
## Results are given on the log odds ratio (not the response) scale. 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 3 estimates
emtrends(Hypo_model2, pairwise ~ URBAN_GRAD, var = "TRIAL") # do slopes differ significantly from each other?
## $emtrends
##  URBAN_GRAD TRIAL.trend    SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
##  LOW              0.132 0.132 Inf   -0.1256     0.390
##  MODERATE         0.136 0.119 Inf   -0.0978     0.370
##  URBAN           -0.170 0.146 Inf   -0.4565     0.117
## 
## Confidence level used: 0.95 
## 
## $contrasts
##  contrast         estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  LOW - MODERATE   -0.00401 0.178 Inf  -0.023  0.9997
##  LOW - URBAN       0.30182 0.197 Inf   1.533  0.2753
##  MODERATE - URBAN  0.30583 0.189 Inf   1.618  0.2378
## 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 3 estimates

 

Test data

Now let’s analyse the test data, usually this is a bit different because you are equally interested in all 10-20 unrewarded choices rather than the effect of trial number. Because of this, we can run an intercept-only model; in logistic regression an intercept only model compares the choice distribution to a 50% chance level. Again, we can see the singular boundary error (see above), again you can choose to keep or simplify the model.

  

bluelm <- glmer(CHOICE~ 1 + (1|BEEID), family = binomial, data = btest)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
summary(bluelm)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: CHOICE ~ 1 + (1 | BEEID)
##    Data: btest
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    138.4    143.8    -67.2    134.4      108 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -1.5275 -1.5275  0.6546  0.6546  0.6546 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  BEEID  (Intercept) 0        0       
## Number of obs: 110, groups:  BEEID, 11
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)   0.8473     0.2081   4.072 4.65e-05 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## optimizer (Nelder_Mead) convergence code: 0 (OK)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
yellowlm <- glmer(CHOICE~ 1 + (1|BEEID), family = binomial, data = ytest) 
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
summary(yellowlm)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: CHOICE ~ 1 + (1 | BEEID)
##    Data: ytest
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    136.8    142.0    -66.4    132.8       98 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -1.2773 -1.2773  0.7829  0.7829  0.7829 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  BEEID  (Intercept) 0        0       
## Number of obs: 100, groups:  BEEID, 10
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
## (Intercept)   0.4895     0.2060   2.376   0.0175 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## optimizer (Nelder_Mead) convergence code: 0 (OK)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
bluelm2 <- glm(CHOICE~ 1, family = binomial, data = btest) # simplified model
summary(bluelm2)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = CHOICE ~ 1, family = binomial, data = btest)
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)   0.8473     0.2081   4.072 4.65e-05 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 134.39  on 109  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 134.39  on 109  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 136.39
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4
yellowlm2 <- glm(CHOICE~ 1, family = binomial, data = ytest) 
summary(yellowlm2)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = CHOICE ~ 1, family = binomial, data = ytest)
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
## (Intercept)   0.4895     0.2060   2.376   0.0175 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 132.81  on 99  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 132.81  on 99  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 134.81
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

 

Let’s calculate confidence intervals which are required for subsequent figures. Note that the output are in the log-scale because of the logit link function, therefore we can reverse the link function to get meaningful values. You do this by first running the confint command to find the confidence intervals. Since we are analysing an intercept only model, we look at the 2.5% and 97.5% confidence bounds for the intercept. We then take this value and input it the reverse link function formula as shown below, do this for both the upper and lower bounds. We then save these values as confintlow or confinthigh so we can use these values in subsequent code.

confint(bluelm) #confidence interval
## Computing profile confidence intervals ...
##                 2.5 %    97.5 %
## .sig01      0.0000000 0.5457291
## (Intercept) 0.4491324 1.2676763
bconfintlow<-(exp(0.4491324)/(1 + exp(0.4491324))) #reverse link function
bconfinthigh<-(exp(1.2676763)/(1 + exp(1.2676763)))
print(bconfinthigh)
## [1] 0.7803447
print(bconfintlow)
## [1] 0.6104329
confint(yellowlm)
## Computing profile confidence intervals ...
##                  2.5 %    97.5 %
## .sig01      0.00000000 0.6013464
## (Intercept) 0.09087126 0.9010195
yconfintlow <-(exp(0.09087126)/(1 + exp(0.09087126)))
yconfinthigh<-(exp(0.9010195)/(1 + exp(0.9010195)))

 

Supplementing your p value

Effect size and Odds ratio

The p value simply tells you the probability of obtaining your data (or more extreme data) given that your null hypothesis is true. Often times you want to supplement your p value with additional information. A common inclusion is the effect size which tells you the strength of the relationship measured. For logistic regression (binomial data) you can’t calculate a traditional effect size (cohen’s d) but you can report the odds-ratio which tells is simply the ratio of the probability of an event and the probability of a non-event. In other words it tells us if there is a strong or weak relationship between two things. Odds ratios that are greater than 1 indicate that the event is more likely to occur as the predictor increases. Odds ratios that are less than 1 indicate that the event is less likely to occur as the predictor increases.In the context of bee experiments the odds ratio will tell us the odds that increasing trial number will increase the probability of a correct. choice. Note that you can only calculate an effect size when you have a predictor in your model. Also, it is important to present confidence intervals alongside the odds ratio to show how much uncertainty there is for that odds ratio.

coef(btraininglm2) # the estimate is the slope, we exp this
## (Intercept)       TRIAL 
##  0.08419834  0.12242142
exp(btraininglm2$coefficients[-1]) # For a one-unit increase in trial, the odds of a correct choice increased by a factor of 1.13.
##   TRIAL 
## 1.13023
# OR as a percentage ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
(exp(coef(btraininglm2))[-1] - 1) * 100 #For a one unit increase in trial number, the odds of a correct choice increases by 13%
##    TRIAL 
## 13.02303
# Confidence intervals of OR

exp(confint(btraininglm2)) # there is uncertainty surrounding an odds ratio so it is important to present the corresponding CI
## Waiting for profiling to be done...
##                 2.5 %   97.5 %
## (Intercept) 0.5729174 2.084640
## TRIAL       1.0617897 1.208988

Bayes Factor

If the alternate hypothesis is rejected we can say that we have failed to reject the null hypothesis. We can’t say that we accept the null hypothesis because our test (Frequentist statistics) does not actually provide evidence supporting the null. In this case we can support our null results with a Bayes factor (Bayesian statistics) which directly tests and compares the evidence for the null and alternate hypothesis. In this way, a Bayes factor can tell us if the data collected is more consistent with the null or alternate hypothesis.

The important thing to note for a Bayes factor is that you start the model with a prior, which is your prior expectation of the result (how likely each hypothesis is). In many cases, you might not have an idea of whether the effect of your predictor will be positive, negative, or zero (null), in which case you can use the default uninformative prior which assumes equal prior probabilities. As a rough guide, a Bayes factor above 3 shows good evidence for the null.

Note: generalized linear mixed models (glmer) is not currently supported by BFpack so if you want to generate a Bayes factor you must simplify the model.

BF_glm <- BF(btraininglm2, hypothesis = "TRIAL = 0; TRIAL > 0", prior.hyp = c(0.5, 0.5, 0)) # We're interested in two hypotheses, the null (0) and the alternate (>0)
summary(BF_glm) # The default setting is prior.hyp = NULL which sets equal prior probabilities AKA uniform prior. 
## Call:
## BF.glm(x = btraininglm2, hypothesis = "TRIAL = 0; TRIAL > 0", 
##     prior.hyp = c(0.5, 0.5, 0))
## 
## Bayesian hypothesis test
## Type: exploratory
## Object: glm
## Parameter: general parameters
## Method: adjusted fractional Bayes factors using Gaussian approximations
## 
## Posterior probabilities
## 
## Model parameters:
##             Pr(=0) Pr(<0) Pr(>0)
## (Intercept)  0.872  0.051  0.077
## TRIAL        0.007  0.000  0.993
## 
## Bayesian hypothesis test
## Type: confirmatory
## Object: glm
## Parameter: general parameters
## Method: adjusted fractional Bayes factors using Gaussian approximations
## 
## Posterior probabilities:
##    Pr(hypothesis|data)
## H1               0.007
## H2               0.993
## H3               0.000
## 
## Evidence matrix (BFs):
##         H1    H2       H3
## H1   1.000 0.007   70.099
## H2 140.539 1.000 9851.690
## H3   0.014 0.000    1.000
## 
## Specification table:
##    complex= complex>  fit= fit>   BF= BF>    BF   PHP
## H1    0.856      1.0 0.012    1 0.014   1 1.014 0.007
## H2    1.000      0.5 1.000    1 1.000   2 7.388 0.993
## H3    1.000      0.5 1.000    0 1.000   0 1.000 0.000
## 
## Hypotheses:
## H1: TRIAL=0
## H2: TRIAL>0
## H3: complement
0.811/0.189 # if you divide the posterior probabilities you also get the Bayes Factor
## [1] 4.291005
BF_glm2 <- BF(bluelm2, hypothesis = "Intercept = 0; Intercept > 0")
summary(BF_glm2)
## Call:
## BF.glm(x = bluelm2, hypothesis = "Intercept = 0; Intercept > 0")
## 
## Bayesian hypothesis test
## Type: exploratory
## Object: glm
## Parameter: general parameters
## Method: adjusted fractional Bayes factors using Gaussian approximations
## 
## Posterior probabilities
## 
## Model parameters:
##             Pr(=0) Pr(<0) Pr(>0)
## (Intercept)  0.001      0  0.999
## 
## Bayesian hypothesis test
## Type: confirmatory
## Object: glm
## Parameter: general parameters
## Method: adjusted fractional Bayes factors using Gaussian approximations
## 
## Posterior probabilities:
##    Pr(hypothesis|data)
## H1               0.001
## H2               0.999
## H3               0.000
## 
## Evidence matrix (BFs):
##         H1    H2        H3
## H1   1.000 0.001    56.452
## H2 761.123 1.000 42966.881
## H3   0.018 0.000     1.000
## 
## Specification table:
##    complex= complex> fit= fit>   BF= BF>    BF   PHP
## H1    0.183      1.0    0    1 0.003   1 1.003 0.001
## H2    1.000      0.5    1    1 1.000   2 7.389 0.999
## H3    1.000      0.5    1    0 1.000   0 1.000 0.000
## 
## Hypotheses:
## H1: Intercept=0
## H2: Intercept>0
## H3: complement

P value uncertainty

P values from identical experiments can differ greatly in a way that is surprising to many. The failure to appreciate this wide variability can lead researchers to expect, without adequate justification, that statistically significant findings will be replicated. If you wish to show this variability you can easily calculate this using the excel calculator provided by this paper: https://www.nature.com/articles/nmeth.3741

Generate figures

Bargraph

In the following code, we generate a bar graph from the test data. First we need to create a data frame so that ggplot can extract information to generate a bar graph. Second we create another data frame called meanpoints where we store the mean value of each bee so that we can plot the mean choices of each individual bee. To edit the first data frame for your own data, you can replace yellow and blue with the different conditions in your experiment, then add the mean values in the proportion column in the same order as how you listed your conditions in the condition column (i.e. if you have three conditions, you will instead have 3 means to list in the proportion column. The same is true for the confidence bounds)

dframe <- data.frame(condition = rep(c("Yellow","Blue")), # Create dataframe where there is a column called condition and rows called yellow and blue
                legend = c("Y","B"), 
                proportion = c(0.62,0.7), # Add a new column called proportion with rows that have the following values, corresponding to the mean of yellow and blue
                confintlow = c(yconfintlow,bconfintlow), # Add confintlow column including the lower confidence bound values for yellow and blue
                confinthigh = c(yconfinthigh,bconfinthigh)) # Add confinthigh column including the upper confidence bound values for yellow and blue

meanpoints <- test %>%   # Calculate mean of each block for each bee
  group_by(BEEID, CONDITION) %>% 
  summarize(prop = mean(CHOICE)) 
## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'BEEID'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
  colnames(meanpoints)[2] ="condition" # rename column to match dframe, because the column name needs to match the dframe as we want to plot both in the same graph
  levels(meanpoints$condition) <- tolower(levels(meanpoints$condition)) # rename condition names to match 
  levels(meanpoints$condition) <- str_to_title(levels(meanpoints$condition)) # recapitalize condition names

Now let’s generate the figure.

testgraph <- ggplot(dframe, aes(x = condition, y = proportion, fill = condition)) +
  labs (x= "Condition", y = "Mean proportion of correct choices", fill="Condition") +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge", width = .7) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey") +
  geom_point(data = meanpoints, aes(x = condition, y = prop), position = position_jitter(width=0.1), size =2, colour="dark grey") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = wes_palette("GrandBudapest2", n = 2)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = confintlow, ymax = confinthigh), width=.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1, suffix = NULL), limits = c(0,1)) +
  theme(panel.background = element_blank(),
        axis.line = element_line(colour = "black"),
        aspect.ratio = 1/1,
        legend.position = "none")
print(testgraph)

Sometimes you want to rearrange the default order of your conditions/factors because ggplot will plot the conditions in ascending order by default, please peruse the following code which may help you with this.

levels(meanpoints$condition) # view the existing order of factors in the meanpoints
levels(dframe$condition)
meanpoints$condition <- factor(meanpoints$condition, levels=c('Yellow', 'Blue')) # set the order of your factors as you wish
levels(meanpoints$condition) # check to view the change

dframe$condition # View the existing order of factors in the dframe
dframe$condition <- factor(dframe$condition, levels = c('Yellow', 'Blue')) # change the order of conditions in your dframe to match if they aren't matching those in meanpoints
dframe$condition # check to view the change

After changing this order, plot the same graph and see how the order of the bars change:

Scatter graph

Alternatively if you don’t like bar graphs you can remove the bars and simply show the error bars

testgraph2 <- ggplot(dframe, aes(x = condition, y = proportion, fill = condition)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey") +
  labs (x= "Condition", y = "Mean proportion of correct choices", fill="Condition") +
  geom_point(data = dframe, aes(x = condition, y = proportion),
             position = position_dodge(width = 0.9), colour = "black") + 
  geom_point(data = meanpoints, aes(x = condition, y = prop, colour = condition), position = position_jitter(width=0.1), size =2) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = confintlow, ymax = confinthigh), width=.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1, suffix = NULL), limits = c(0,1)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("#89CFF0", "#E4D00A")) +
  theme(panel.background = element_blank(),
        axis.line = element_line(colour = "black"),
        aspect.ratio = 1/1,
        legend.position = "none")
print(testgraph2)

Line graph

We can graphically represent the training data as a line graph. First create the dataframe for ggplot to extract information to generate a line graph.

## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'BEEID'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
## tibble [4 × 3] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
##  $ BLOCK     : Factor w/ 4 levels "5","10","15",..: 1 2 3 4
##  $ proportion: num [1:4] 0.64 0.68 0.84 0.94
##  $ sd        : num [1:4] 0.0843 0.1687 0.1838 0.0966

Now generate the figure. Note if the error bars look weird it’s usually because it goes outside the y axi, in which case just extend the y axis slightly.

learningcurve <- ggplot(blockmean,  
                            aes(x = BLOCK, 
                                y = prop,
                                group = factor(BEEID)))+ 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey") + 
  geom_jitter(size = 1, alpha = 0.5, width = 0.1, height = 0.01, colour = blockmean$BEEID) +
  geom_line(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.2, colour = blockmean$BEEID) +
  geom_point(data = blockmean,
             aes(x = "5",
                 y = allmean$proportion[1]), size = 2) +
  geom_point(data = blockmean,
             aes(x = "10",
                 y = allmean$proportion[2]), size = 2) +
  geom_point(data = blockmean,
             aes(x = "15",
                 y = allmean$proportion[3]), size = 2) +
   geom_point(data = blockmean,
             aes(x = "20",
                 y = allmean$proportion[4]), size = 2) +
  geom_errorbar(data = blockmean,
                aes( x = "5", 
                     ymin = allmean$proportion[1] - allmean$sd[1], 
                     ymax = allmean$proportion[1] + allmean$sd[1]), width = .2) +
  geom_errorbar(data = blockmean,
                aes( x = "10",
                     ymin = allmean$proportion[2] - allmean$sd[2], 
                     ymax = allmean$proportion[2] + allmean$sd[2]), width = .2) +
  geom_errorbar(data = blockmean,
                aes( x = "15",
                     ymin = allmean$proportion[3] - allmean$sd[3], 
                     ymax = allmean$proportion[3] + allmean$sd[3]), width = .2) +
   geom_errorbar(data = blockmean,
                aes( x = "20",
                     ymin = allmean$proportion[4] - allmean$sd[4], 
                     ymax = allmean$proportion[4] + allmean$sd[4]), width = .2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1, suffix = NULL), limits = c(0,1.05)) + # suffix = NULL to remove % on the y labels
  xlab("Trial") +     
  ylab("Mean proportion of correct choice") +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14),  
        axis.text.x  = element_text(size = 10, colour = "black"),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14, vjust = 1),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10, colour = "black")) +
  scale_color_gradient() +
  theme(panel.background = element_blank(),
        axis.line = element_line(colour = "black"),
        aspect.ratio = 1/1)
## Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
## ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.
print(learningcurve)

Figure with predicted values

As seen above, the line graph often looks messy. What we often do in publications is produce a figure based on the predicted values of our regression model.The first thing we need to do is generate predicted data points. Importantly you cannot do this for intercept only models because you need to have trial/choice number in the model.

choicenumlm  <- data.frame (TRIAL = seq(1,20, length.out = 20)) # Create dataframe from 1 to 20 (20 was the max trial number)
Xgc1 <- model.matrix(~TRIAL, data = choicenumlm) # Create matrix of choice number
coeff <- c(as.numeric(btraininglm2$coefficients[1]), as.numeric(btraininglm2$coefficients[2])) # Create vector of model coefficients pulled from lm1
eta <- Xgc1 %*% coeff # Calculate predicted mean proportion of correct choices
SE <- sqrt(diag(Xgc1 %*% vcov(btraininglm2) %*% t(Xgc1))) # Calculate standard errors

Now that we have x, y values, we can create a dataframe for ggplot

PREDLM <- data.frame (TRIAL = choicenumlm$TRIAL, ETA  = eta, STDERR = SE) # Create and add to a new dataframe
PREDLM$CONFINTLOW <-(PREDLM$ETA - 1.96*PREDLM$STDERR)  # Calculate confint lower bound
PREDLM$CONFINTHIGH <-(PREDLM$ETA + 1.96*PREDLM$STDERR) # Calculate confint upper bound
PREDLM$EXPETA <- (exp(PREDLM$ETA)/(1 + exp(PREDLM$ETA))) # Reverse the link function of the predicted mean proportion of correct choices
PREDLM$EXPCONFINTLOW <- (exp(PREDLM$CONFINTLOW)/(1 + exp(PREDLM$CONFINTLOW))) # Reverse the link function of the lower bound
PREDLM$EXPCONFINTHIGH <- (exp(PREDLM$CONFINTHIGH)/(1 +exp(PREDLM$CONFINTHIGH))) # Reverse the link function of the upper bound

And then generate a figure using ggplot using this dataframe

Regfig <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = TRIAL, y = EXPETA), size = 0.5, data = PREDLM) +
  
  annotate("point", x = 5,  y = allmean$proportion[1], size = 2) +
  annotate("point", x = 10, y = allmean$proportion[2], size = 2) +
  annotate("point", x = 15, y = allmean$proportion[3], size = 2) +
  
  annotate("errorbar",
           x = 5,
           ymin = allmean$proportion[1] - allmean$sd[1],
           ymax = allmean$proportion[1] + allmean$sd[1],
           width = 0.2) +
  
  annotate("errorbar",
           x = 10,
           ymin = allmean$proportion[2] - allmean$sd[2],
           ymax = allmean$proportion[2] + allmean$sd[2],
           width = 0.2) +
  
  annotate("errorbar",
           x = 15,
           ymin = allmean$proportion[3] - allmean$sd[3],
           ymax = allmean$proportion[3] + allmean$sd[3],
           width = 0.2) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1.05)) +
  
  geom_ribbon(aes(x = TRIAL,
                  ymin = EXPCONFINTLOW,
                  ymax = EXPCONFINTHIGH),
              fill = "purple", alpha = 0.3,
              data = PREDLM) +
  
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "gray96")) +
  
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
        axis.title.y = element_text(margin = margin(0,20,0,10)),
        axis.title.x = element_text(margin = margin(20,0,10,0))) +
  
  labs(x = "Choice number",
       y = "Mean proportion of correct choices (%)")

print(Regfig)

What if you want to plot multiple models in one figure? Simply calculate the model predictions independently and then plot them together in ggplot. Below we will calculate the predicted values of the yellow training model and plot them alongside the previous predicted values. We will add the new data to the previous dataframe for ggplot.

coeff2 <- c(as.numeric(ytraininglm2$coefficients[1]), as.numeric(ytraininglm2$coefficients[2])) # Create vector of model coefficients pulled from lm2
eta2 <- Xgc1 %*% coeff2 # Calculate predicted mean proportion of correct choices
SE2 <- sqrt(diag(Xgc1 %*% vcov(ytraininglm2) %*% t(Xgc1))) # Calculate standard errors


PREDLM$ETA2 <- eta2 # Add mean proportion of correct choices to existing dataframe
PREDLM$STDERR2 <- SE2 # Add standard error to existing dataframe
PREDLM$CONFINTLOW2 <- (PREDLM$ETA2 - 1.96*PREDLM$STDERR2) # Calculate confint lower bound 
PREDLM$CONFINTHIGH2 <- (PREDLM$ETA2 + 1.96*PREDLM$STDERR2) # Calculate confint upper bound
PREDLM$EXPETA2 <- (exp(PREDLM$ETA2)/(1 + exp(PREDLM$ETA2))) # Reverse the link function of the predicted mean proportion of correct choices
PREDLM$EXPCONFINTLOW2 <- (exp(PREDLM$CONFINTLOW2)/(1 + exp(PREDLM$CONFINTLOW2))) # Reverse the link function of the lower bound
PREDLM$EXPCONFINTHIGH2 <- (exp(PREDLM$CONFINTHIGH2)/(1 +exp(PREDLM$CONFINTHIGH2))) # Reverse the link function of the upper bound


blockmean2 <- ytraining %>%   # calculate mean of each block for each bee
  group_by(BEEID, BLOCK) %>% 
  summarize(prop = mean(CHOICE))
## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'BEEID'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
allmean2 <- blockmean2 %>%  #calculate mean of each block for all bees
  group_by(BLOCK) %>% 
  summarise(proportion = mean(prop), sd = sd(prop))
str(allmean2)
## tibble [4 × 3] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
##  $ BLOCK     : Factor w/ 4 levels "5","10","15",..: 1 2 3 4
##  $ proportion: num [1:4] 0.56 0.62 0.62 0.56
##  $ sd        : num [1:4] 0.126 0.148 0.114 0.184
Regfig2 <- ggplot() +

  geom_line(aes(x = TRIAL, y = EXPETA), size = 0.5, data = PREDLM) +
  geom_line(aes(x = TRIAL, y = EXPETA2, linetype = "dashed"),
            size = 0.5, data = PREDLM) +

  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1.05)) +

  geom_ribbon(aes(x = TRIAL,
                  ymin = EXPCONFINTLOW,
                  ymax = EXPCONFINTHIGH),
              fill = "blue", alpha = 0.3,
              data = PREDLM) +

  geom_ribbon(aes(x = TRIAL,
                  ymin = EXPCONFINTLOW2,
                  ymax = EXPCONFINTHIGH2),
              fill = "gold", alpha = 0.3,
              data = PREDLM) +

  # ---------------- BLUE MODEL ----------------
  annotate("point", x = 4.7,  y = allmean$proportion[1], size = 2, color = "blue3") + # manually putting the error bars at these locations on the figure
  annotate("point", x = 9.7,  y = allmean$proportion[2], size = 2, color = "blue3") +
  annotate("point", x = 14.7, y = allmean$proportion[3], size = 2, color = "blue3") +

  annotate("errorbar",
           x = 4.7,
           ymin = allmean$proportion[1] - allmean$sd[1],
           ymax = allmean$proportion[1] + allmean$sd[1],
           width = 0.2) +

  annotate("errorbar",
           x = 9.7,
           ymin = allmean$proportion[2] - allmean$sd[2],
           ymax = allmean$proportion[2] + allmean$sd[2],
           width = 0.2) +

  annotate("errorbar",
           x = 14.7,
           ymin = allmean$proportion[3] - allmean$sd[3],
           ymax = allmean$proportion[3] + allmean$sd[3],
           width = 0.2) +

  # ---------------- GOLD MODEL ----------------
  annotate("point", x = 5.3,  y = allmean2$proportion[1], size = 2, color = "darkgoldenrod4") + # manually putting the error bars at these locations on the figure
  annotate("point", x = 10.3, y = allmean2$proportion[2], size = 2, color = "darkgoldenrod4") +
  annotate("point", x = 15.3, y = allmean2$proportion[3], size = 2, color = "darkgoldenrod4") +

  annotate("errorbar",
           x = 5.3,
           ymin = allmean2$proportion[1] - allmean2$sd[1],
           ymax = allmean2$proportion[1] + allmean2$sd[1],
           width = 0.2) +

  annotate("errorbar",
           x = 10.3,
           ymin = allmean2$proportion[2] - allmean2$sd[2],
           ymax = allmean2$proportion[2] + allmean2$sd[2],
           width = 0.2) +

  annotate("errorbar",
           x = 15.3,
           ymin = allmean2$proportion[3] - allmean2$sd[3],
           ymax = allmean2$proportion[3] + allmean2$sd[3],
           width = 0.2) +

  # theme
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "gray96"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.title.y = element_text(margin = margin(0,20,0,10)),
    axis.title.x = element_text(margin = margin(20,0,10,0))
  ) +

  labs(x = "Choice number",
       y = "Mean proportion of correct choices (%)") +

  scale_linetype_identity(guide = "legend",
                          labels = c("Yellow", "Blue"))

print(Regfig2)

Further detail on ggplot2

ggplot2 is simple to understand. Let’s go through each line of the bar graph. First you need to define the dataframe you want to plot in ggplot2. We then use “aes” to tell ggplot how you want the variables to match to different aesthetics/visual properties. This means that if you have two conditions for example, ggplot will know to allocate two different colours to represent each condition. NOTE: You can also manually define colours outside of “aes”, if you do this ggplot will not automatically map these colours to an aesthetic.

testgraph <- ggplot(dframe, aes(x = condition, y = proportion, fill = condition))
print(testgraph)

You can see that ggplot has plotted the space for your data, now you can add geoms which are the different layers of commands for your graph. Let’s add the bars and x/y labels, we do this by simplying adding a “+” at the end of each line.

testgraph <- ggplot(dframe, aes(x = condition, y = proportion, fill = condition)) +
  labs (x= "Condition", y = "Mean proportion of correct choices", fill="Condition") +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge", width = .7) # stat identity tells ggplot that you are provided your own y values for the bar heights
print(testgraph) # dodge tells ggplot how far apart you want your bars

Let’s continue to add more geoms, let’s add error bars and raw data. Notice that you can map individual aesthetics for each geom type. Also note that we apply jitter to the raw data in geom_point to tell ggplot to spread out the points (i.e. jittering) slightly instead of overlapping them on top of each other.

testgraph <- ggplot(dframe, aes(x = condition, y = proportion, fill = condition)) +
  labs (x= "Condition", y = "Mean proportion of correct choices", fill="Condition") +
   geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey") +
  geom_point(data = meanpoints, aes(x = condition, y = prop), position = position_jitter(width=0.1), size =2, colour="dark grey") +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge", width = .7) + 
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = confintlow, ymax = confinthigh), width=.2)
print(testgraph)

It seems the the bars and grey dotted line are hiding some of the data. This is because ggplot maps each layer sequentially, if we move the geom point layer below the geom bar then ggplot will graph the points last on top of the bars. Let’s also add a custom colour palette to the graph, we can specify this using scale_fill_manual to tell ggplot to extract 2 (i.e. n = 2) colours from this palette to map to aesthetics. NOTE: alternatively you can specify exact hex colours (see scatter graph section).

testgraph <- ggplot(dframe, aes(x = condition, y = proportion, fill = condition)) +
  labs (x= "Condition", y = "Mean proportion of correct choices", fill="Condition") +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge", width = .7) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey") +
  geom_point(data = meanpoints, aes(x = condition, y = prop), position = position_jitter(width=0.1), size =2,        colour="dark grey") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = wes_palette("GrandBudapest2", n = 2)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = confintlow, ymax = confinthigh), width=.2) 
print(testgraph)

Nice! Finally let’s try and make the overall graph look nicer. We can use geom_text to add a significance asterix at a specific coordinate on the graph (in this graph its not necessary but you can use this code when needed). We can also specify overall aesthetics requirements of the graph using “theme”, for example we use panel.background to remove the default background of the graph. We also remove the legend which we don’t need here.

testgraph <- ggplot(dframe, aes(x = condition, y = proportion, fill = condition)) +
  labs (x= "Condition", y = "Mean proportion of correct choices", fill="Condition") +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge", width = .7) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey") +
  geom_point(data = meanpoints, aes(x = condition, y = prop), position = position_jitter(width=0.1), size =2,        colour="dark grey") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = wes_palette("GrandBudapest2", n = 2)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = confintlow, ymax = confinthigh), width=.2) +
  geom_text(x="Blue", y = 0.9, label ="*", size =10) +
  geom_text(x="Yellow", y = 0.9, label ="*", size =10) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1, suffix = NULL), limits = c(0,1)) +
  theme(panel.background = element_blank(),
        axis.line = element_line(colour = "black"),
        aspect.ratio = 1/1,
        legend.position = "none")
print(testgraph)

Use the ggThemeAssist add-in to play around with the aesthetics. This add-in helps you customize your graph aesthetics and then automatically adds the code to your graph. You can view this code to better understand how ggplot works. Highlight the code and then click Add-ins and click on ggplot Theme Assistant. Now hopefully you have a good understanding of how ggplot works! All other graph types follow a similar principle, try removing individual lines from the above regression graphs to see what changes in the figure.

Simulation-based power analysis

If you want to run a power analysis, I recommend simulation-based analysis, where you generate simulated datasets around a true effect. THen models are generated for each dataset to see if they can detect this true effect. We then calculate the percentage of models that succesffully detected the effect versus those that did not. Generally we want to detect the true effect above 80% of the time.

# Parameters
n_bees <- 20 # how many bees
n_data_points <- 10 # how many data points
n_total <- n_bees * n_data_points
p1 <- 0.65  # What is the true effect you are trying to detect? Let's say learning of 65% accuracy
alpha <- 0.05
nsim <- 100  # Number of simulations, generally set to 1000 but here I did 100 only

# Function to simulate data with random effects
simulate_data <- function(n_bees, n_data_points, p, random_effect_sd) {
  bee_ids <- rep(1:n_bees, each = n_data_points)
  random_effects <- rnorm(n_bees, 0, random_effect_sd)
  choices <- sapply(1:n_total, function(i) {
    prob <- plogis(qlogis(p) + random_effects[bee_ids[i]])
    rbinom(1, 1, prob)
  })
  data.frame(bee_id = factor(bee_ids), choice = choices)
}

# General assumptions for random effect standard deviation
random_effect_sd <- 0.4418846 # aprior random effect standard deviation, you can just take a guess or check the values of previous models/experiments.

# Simulate data with the specified effect size
set.seed(123)  # For reproducibility
simulated_data <- simulate_data(n_bees, n_data_points, p1, random_effect_sd)

# Print the simulated dataframe
print(head(simulated_data))
##   bee_id choice
## 1      1      1
## 2      1      1
## 3      1      1
## 4      1      1
## 5      1      1
## 6      1      1
summary(simulated_data)
##      bee_id        choice     
##  1      : 10   Min.   :0.000  
##  2      : 10   1st Qu.:0.000  
##  3      : 10   Median :1.000  
##  4      : 10   Mean   :0.685  
##  5      : 10   3rd Qu.:1.000  
##  6      : 10   Max.   :1.000  
##  (Other):140
# Initialize counter for significant results
significant_results <- 0

# Run simulations
for (i in 1:nsim) {
  # Simulate data under the alternative hypothesis
  simulated_data <- simulate_data(n_bees, n_data_points, p1, random_effect_sd)
  
  # Fit the model to the simulated data, make sure the model structure is correct
  model <- tryCatch({
    glmer(choice ~ 1 + (1 | bee_id), family = binomial(link = "logit"), data = simulated_data)
  }, error = function(e) {
    message("Error fitting model: ", e)
    return(NULL)
  })
  
  if (!is.null(model)) {
    # Extract p-value for the intercept
    p_value <- summary(model)$coefficients[1, "Pr(>|z|)"]
    
    # Check if p-value is below the significance level
    if (p_value < alpha) {
      significant_results <- significant_results + 1
    }
  }
}
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
# Calculate power
power_estimate <- significant_results / nsim
print(paste("Estimated Power: ", round(power_estimate, 3)))
## [1] "Estimated Power:  0.92"
